coredata.dc:title: Surface-engineered N-doped carbon nanotubes with B-doped graphene quantum dots: Strategies to develop highly-efficient noble metal-free electrocatalyst for online-monitoring dissolved oxygen biosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yu-Xuan
Rinawati, Mia
Huang, Wei-Hsiang
Cheng, Yao-Sheng

Su, Wei-Nien
Yeh, Min-Hsin
coredata.dc:description: Dissolved oxygen (DO) is an essential indicator for evaluating water quality, the exquisitely sensitive electrochemical DO sensor could accurately record response which was based on oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) with a sluggish reaction rate. Thus, high-efficient ORR electrocatalysts are urgently needed for broad applications. As a matter of fact, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted a great deal of attention, several heteroatoms are extensively introduced in GQDs fragments to regulate their inherent chemical and electrical properties by adjusting their electronic structure. However, B–N–C moieties always exist for some BN-codoped carbons due to the intermolecular force that might prohibit its electrocatalytic activity. Therefore, this research design a strategy to solve this problem; despite substantial efforts, this study shows a metal-free B-doped GQD/N-doped carbon nanotubes (BGQD/NCNTs) electrode significantly influences ORR electrocatalytic activity due to the synergistic effect and abundant active sites. Herein, BGQD/NCNTs exhibit an excellent ORR performance with an onset potential of 0.91 V (vs. RHE), exceeding most reported GQDs-introduced electrocatalysts; it also outperformed the commercial electrocatalyst in terms of long-term stability. For seawater DO sensing, BGQD/NCNTs exhibit ultra-high sensitivity of 0.011 mA/cm2ppm and further integrate into an online detection platform to accomplish the aim of continuous monitoring via a wireless connection.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dissolved oxygen (DO) is an essential indicator for evaluating water quality, the exquisitely sensitive electrochemical DO sensor could accurately record response which was based on oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) with a sluggish reaction rate. Thus, high-efficient ORR electrocatalysts are urgently needed for broad applications. As a matter of fact, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted a great deal of attention, several heteroatoms are extensively introduced in GQDs fragments to regulate their inherent chemical and electrical properties by adjusting their electronic structure. However, B–N–C moieties always exist for some BN-codoped carbons due to the intermolecular force that might prohibit its electrocatalytic activity. Therefore, this research design a strategy to solve this problem; despite substantial efforts, this study shows a metal-free B-doped GQD/N-doped carbon nanotubes (BGQD/NCNTs) electrode significantly influences ORR electrocatalytic activity due to the synergistic effect and abundant active sites. Herein, BGQD/NCNTs exhibit an excellent ORR performance with an onset potential of 0.91 V (vs. RHE), exceeding most reported GQDs-introduced electrocatalysts; it also outperformed the commercial electrocatalyst in terms of long-term stability. For seawater DO sensing, BGQD/NCNTs exhibit ultra-high sensitivity of 0.011 mA/cm2ppm and further integrate into an online detection platform to accomplish the aim of continuous monitoring via a wireless connection.

ce:section.ce:para: Dissolved oxygen (DO) corresponds to non-compound oxygen in a solution involving diverse physiological and biochemical activities [1]. For instance, DO is an important indication of water quality and purification, indicating the environment's ability to self-regulate. High DO is attributed to the breakdown of different contaminants in water, implying that the aqueous solution can be purified rapidly. In the field of aquaculture fishery, a low DO concentration causes the sluggish breakdown of contaminants in water and is unsuitable for fish spawning and growth [2]. Therefore, DO measurement is important in lots of applications [3] including biomedical fields, food production, aquaculture fishery, industrial and agriculture production [4,5]. Nowadays, the primary determination methods of DO include iodometric [6], electrochemical and optical methods [7]. Iodometric titration is a conventional laboratory analytical chemistry method for determining DO level that serves as a globally accepted benchmark method due to its excellent accuracy [8]. However, it is restricted by its time-consuming detection techniques and inability to provide continuous online detection. The optical dissolved oxygen sensors are based on a fluorescence quenching concept that does not consume oxygen, making sensor miniaturization with excellent anti-interference ability but poor precision possible [9]. Therefore, electrochemical detection is the most widely used method among various DO measurement strategies because of its rapid response, high sensitivity and easy operation [10,11]. However, because the electrochemical DO measuring mechanism is based on the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), the oxygen concentration may be evaluated by analyzing the oxygen reduction current, which has a positive correlation with DO value [12]. In order to fabricate a electrochemical sensor with outstanding sensitivity for DO monitoring, it is highly desirable to exploit a promising electrocatalyst toward ORR in the board pH range.

To date, Pt-based nanomaterial is extensively employed as state-of-the-art electrocatalysts for ORR, nevertheless, prohibitive cost, the scarcity, and unsatisfying endurance owing to its poor methanol crossover tolerance significantly hinders its wide utilization [13,14]. Alternatively, various efforts have been dedicated to exploring non-noble metals and metal-free electrocatalyst for the replacement of Pt-based electrocatalysts [15–20]. Recently, carbon materials as electrocatalysts for ORR have drawn enormous interest owing to their superior durability, low cost, excellent CO and methanol tolerance compared to Pt-based electrocatalysts [21,22]. Notably, carbon nanomaterial including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene oxides (GOs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and porous carbon with superior permanence and electrocatalytic activity toward ORR have been attracted a great deal of attention [23,24]. Zero-dimensional nanosized graphene fragments of GQDs with their diameters around 5–20 nm have fascinated massive interests for outstanding efficiency toward noble metal-free electrocatalysts toward ORR due to their chemical inertness, high electrical conductivity and large surface area to modulate ORR performance [25]. In addition, GQDs exhibit edge effects and quantum confinement, the edge effect of GQDs plays an essential role in electrocatalysis as electrochemically catalyzed reactions happen at underlying affluent edge sites instead of basal planes. Moreover, its environmentally friendly, non-toxic, and naturally inactive nature is also a major attraction for scientists [26,27].

Several strategies had been developed, including heteroatoms (S, N, P and B) doping into carbon materials to modify their intrinsic chemical and electrical characteristics by modulating the electronic structure of carbon [28–32]. Because B atoms have a lower electronegativity than C atoms, thus have a positive charge. The difference in electronegativity between B and O atoms allows for the easy adsorption of O2 molecules on B atoms, which leads to the subsequent O2 reduction [33]. Therefore, as-synthesized of B-doped nanocarbon might be an impressive strategy for metal-free carbon-based electrocatalysts toward ORR. On the other hand, the synthesis of N-doped carbon material has been extensively investigated [34]. It has been reported that vertically aligned nitrogen-doped carbon nanotube arrays exhibit strong electrocatalytic activity for ORR and enhance the adsorption of O2 molecules on the active center, therefore weakening the oxygen bonding and lowering the ORR barrier. Compared to single-atom doping, co-doping materials (e.g. N,S, N,B-, and N,P-) were proposed to exhibit excellent electrocatalytic performance than single-doping materials towards ORR through synergistic effects [35]. However, inactivated boron nitride (B–N) bonding species are always present in B–N co-doping materials, which would prevent ORR performance owing to the neutral effect [36,37].

Herein, this research proposes a strategy to solve this problem and further facilitate ORR performance. Herein, CNTs were first chosen as the matrix due to their high conductivity and excellent stability, followed by a further pyrolysis treatment at 800 °C for homogeneous N doping [38]. Simultaneously, BGQDs which could provide oxygen-rich functional groups as active sites was synthesized via a hydrothermal process. Subsequently, BGQDs were adsorbed on NCNTs strongly by stirring and ultrasonication due to π-π stacking [39] and further improving electrocatalytic ability as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Particularly, it could significantly diminish the probability of the B–N bonding formation and additionally provide a promising electrocatalyst for DO sensing. Moreover, this research would integrate with the wireless communication device to achieve online DO measurement which could accomplish the continuously remote measurement. Above all, this research might pave the way for designing and constructing a low-cost highly effective electrocatalyst for ORR, which is favorable to practical use for online DO monitoring.

Boric acid (H3BO3, ≥99.8%), D-(+)-glucose (C6H12O6, ≥99.5%), monosodium phosphate (NaH2PO4, ≥98.0%), sodium chloride (NaCl, ≥99.9%), sodium sulfite (Na2O3S, ≥99.9%), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.1%), platinum on graphitized carbon (Pt/C, 20%), Nafion®117 (∼5.0%) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA, ≥99.5%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4, ≥98.0%) and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 28–30%) were obtained from J.T.Baker were used. Carbon nanotube (CNTs, ≥95.0%) were acquired from Uni-Region Bio-Tech. In addition, methanol (MeOH, ≥99.9%) and ethanol (EtOH, ≥99.8%) were stocked from Fluka. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, ≥95.0%) and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%) were from Scharlau. Nitric acid (HNO3, 65%) was purchased from PanReac Applichem. Potassium hydroxide (KOH, ≥98.0%) was bought from Fisher Chemical.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of boron-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: First, 4 g (0.022 mol) of glucose would be dissolved in 40 mL deionized water (DI), followed by adding 16 mg boric acid (0.4 wt%). After that, the mixture dissolved in solution at room temperature (∼25 °C) completely, it was then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and treated hydrothermally for 3 h at 160 °C [22]. After cooling to room temperature, the light yellow solution was produced and the resulting solution was dialyzed by using a 3 k Da dialysis tube (Spectrum Lab. Inc.) to detach unreacted chemicals and impurities.

Nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes were obtained by using the post-treatment method. Initially, we functionalized the as-received CNT for 1 g with 100 mL HNO3 on the reflux system and heated it at 150 °C for 12 h [40]. After that, we neutralized the CNT and acid mixture with ammonia. The functionalized CNT was then washed with a 1:1 vol ratio of ethanol and deionized (DI) water several times and dried overnight at 70 °C. After that, the previously oxidized carbon nanotubes were put into a tube furnace and annealed with a mixed gas of ammonia gas and argon under the temperature of 800 °C for 6 h with the heating rate of 5 °C/min under 10% volume of NH3 in Ar gas.

Briefly, 5 mg as-prepared NCNTs were dispersed in 5 mL water and then were added in 0.25/0.5/0.75 mL as-synthesized B-GQDs solution. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 1 h and stirred overnight. Subsequently, the mixture was then centrifuged at 16,000 rpm and washed with a large amount of water and dried in a box oven at 60 °C overnight.

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images and energy-dispersed X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy were acquired on a scanning electron microscope (JSM-6500F, JEOL) with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS, detector model 7418, Oxford) to observe the morphology and composition of the prepared nanomaterials. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed by using a cold field-emission TEM (JEM 2100F, JEOL) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern were acquired using a D2 Bruker with a monochromatized X-ray diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5418 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was conducted to analyze chemical state using the X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (JPS-9030, JEOL). The X-ray source was 1486.6 eV monochromated Al Kα (X-ray energy of 12 kV, 72 W) and the spot size was 400 μm. The Raman spectra was carried out to define the defect inside materials with a micro Raman system (UniNanoTech UniRAM) equipped with a wavelength of 532 nm and a CCD detector. The spectra was acquired at room temperature. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was conducted on the aqueous as-prepared dispersion of B-GQD at the room temperature. Excitation and emission spectra were acquired by a commercial spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Nanolog-3 spectrofluorometer) equipped with an InGaAs NIR detector and a 20 nm bandpass for both emission and excitation. The PL spectra were scaled according to the measured excitation power.

All electrochemical measurements were performed in Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat (PGSTAT302 N with FRA32 M, Metrohm Autolab). In ORR, a typical three-electrode system was used with Pt wire as a counter electrode, Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) as reference and glass carbon rotating disk electrode (RDE, area = 0.196 cm2) as working electrode. The conversion from Ag/AgCl to RHE used the following equation: ERHE = EAg/AgCl/3M KCl + 0.059 pH + E° Ag/AgCl/3M KCl. The catalyst suspension was prepared by dispersing 10 mg of catalysts in 1 mL mixed-solution with 50 μL Nafion, 750 μL IPA, and 200 μL DI water. Then 10 μL of the electrocatalyst slurry was drop-coated on the electrode surface of RDE and air-dried at room temperature. In addition, the electrolyte was saturated by gauging the gas of nitrogen and oxygen continuously for 30 min before testing in the cell. The electrochemical experiments were carried out in nitrogen/oxygen saturated 0.1 M KOH (pH = 13), 0.01 M PBS (pH = 7), and 0.1 M HClO4 (pH = 0.5) as alkaline, neutral and acidic electrolyte respectively for the ORR test at room temperature. The electrocatalytic activity towards ORR and H2O2 yield was evaluated by performing cyclic voltammetry (CV) with the scan rate of 50 mV/s and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) with the scan rate of 3 mV/s at rotating speeds from 400 to 2500 rpm on RDE and rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE). The electron transferred number was calculated using the Koutecky-Levich equation and extracted from LSV with different rotational speeds [41]. The formula of the K-L equation used is shown below as Equation (1).

The following descriptions elaborate the parameters used in the above equations; i is the obtained current density, ω is the rotational speed of RDE, F is the Faraday number (96485 C mol−1), n is the electron-transferred number, and k is the electron-transfer rate constant. The value of the constants in 0.1 M KOH is 1.2 × 10−6 mol cm−3 for the concentration of O2 in bulk solution (CO), 1.9 × 10-5 cm2 s−1 for the diffusivity of oxygen (DO), and 0.01 cm2 s for the kinetic viscosity of the electrolyte (ʋ).

A similar three electrode system was used in DO measurement but with glass carbon electrode (GCE, area = 0.069 cm2) as working electrode. 0.7 M NaCl was prepared to simulate the condition of seawater with pH value of 8.4 and salinity of 36.2‰. The catalyst suspension was prepared by dispersing 3 mg of catalysts 1 mL mixed-solution with 50 μL Nafion, 750 μL IPA, and 200 μL DI water. Then 4 μL of as-prepared catalyst slurry was drop-coated on the electrode surface of GCE and air-dried at room temperature. CV with the scan rate of 50 mV/s and LSV with the scan rate of 3 mV/s were first performed to realize the ability of DO measurement preliminary in the linear oxygen concentration range of 0–18 mg/L under the temperature of 25 °C. Amperometry (i-t) was conducted to determine the sensitivity for DO measurement with a constant potential of 0.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl 3 M KCl) with the linear range of 0–18 mg/L under the temperature of 25 °C. As the reference, the dissolved oxygen concentration was monitored by commercial DO meter (HQ40D, HACH), and salinity was defined by AZ8373 from AZ Instrument Corp.

Herein, the homemade device from Mighty Electronic Company was acquired for online measurement. The BGQDs/NCNTs based DO meter is shown in Fig. S1 with a two electrode system in 1 M KCl with pH value of 6 and salinity of 76.1‰ under the temperature of 25 °C. Screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) with polyethylene terephthalate (PET) as the matrix were applied as working electrode (area = 0.53 cm2), the electrode should be activated first in 1 M NaOH by performing CV with the scan rate of 50 mV/s in the potential window of 0–0.7 V, and the Ag wire as a counter electrode. The catalyst suspension was prepared by dispersing 3 mg of catalysts 1 mL mixed-solution with 50 μL Nafion, 750 μL IPA, and 200 μL DI water. Then 20 μL of as-prepared catalyst slurry was drop-coated on the electrode surface of SPCE and air-dried at room temperature. After assembling this device with modified SPCE and connect to wifi, the value of DO and temperature would upload to internet pages and achieve the wireless and continuous measurement.

Firstly, BGQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal process with precursors of boric acid and glucose in this work [22]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of BGQDs in Fig. 1 a displayed as-synthesized BGQDs are monodispersed nanoparticles with a uniform diameter of 5.2
±
1.5 nm, suggesting that BGQDs was synthesized successfully. Moreover, high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (Fig. S2) of as-synthesized BGQDs revealed clear lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm, slightly larger than (100) lattice constant of graphene layers (0.21 nm) [43,44] that correspond to the doping effect of B atoms to the graphene backbone, leading to somewhat disordered structure [22]. Furthermore, the most distinguishing aspect of BGQDs is their unique photoluminescence (PL) activity, which could be characterized by recording excitation-emission-intensity spectra, as shown in Fig. 1b. The results demonstrated that the strongest emission wavelength of BGQDs is located in the range of 425–575 nm, identified the BGQDs exhibiting high fluorescence due to the strong quantum effect and edge effect. Moreover, BGQDs aqueous solution displays light-yellow under visible light and emitted green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, as an inset of Fig. 1b shown. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was carried out to explore the binding states of C and B in BGQDs in Fig. 1c and d. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s in Fig. 1c reveals the peak of C 1s is centered at 284.5 eV, the deconvoluted spectra include a peak at 283.5, 284.5, 285.4 and 286.6 eV which is assigned to the chemical bonding of C–B, CC, C–O and CO respectively. Furthermore, two prominent peaks for the high-resolution XPS spectrum of B 1s in Fig. 1d at 190.5 and 191.3 eV are ascribed as the BC2O and BCO2 bonding, verifying that BGQDs contain abundant oxygen-rich functional groups which could facilitate electrocatalytic performance. In addition, the atomic ratio of boron composition was also calculated from XPS results which accounts for 4.86%, indicating successful doping of B into the GQDs carbon lattice. On the other hand, before N-doping process, pristine CNTs were treated with concentrated HNO3 to introduce carboxylic and hydroxyl functional groups into CNTs. The introduction of the carboxylic functional group with hydrophilic properties is attempted to enhance the dispersity of CNTs in.

the solution [45]. NCNTs were prepared as carbon matrix by annealing under ammonia gas and the characterization of NCNTs was provided in Fig. S3. Typical SEM images of acid-treatment CNTs and NCNTs are shown in Fig. S3. The SEM and TEM results show the structure of CNTs is well-maintained even after the pyrolysis process. In addition, EDX reveals the atomic ratio of N for NCNTs is around 0.5% which is consistent with XPS results, suggesting that NCNTs were prepared successfully via the pyrolysis procedure.

The synthesis of BGQD/NCNTs was achieved by physically mixing NCNTs into BGQDs aqueous solutions via ultrasonication and stirring overnight, then the final products were separated by centrifugation. After that, BGQDs were adsorbed on NCNTs strongly due to π-π stacking. TEM image of BGQD/NCNTs in Fig. 2 c clearly demonstrated the successful conjugation between BGQDs and NCNTs. Moreover, pointed out by XPS survey scan, as shown in Fig. S4(a), the respective C 1s, B 1s, N 1s, and O 1s peak were observed, indicating the successful anchorage of BGQDs onto NCNTs. The absence of other components indicates the excellent purity of the as-synthesized material. The chemical elements and their corresponding chemical states for BGQD/NCNTs were investigated by XPS in Fig. 2a and b. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s shows the pyridinic N at 398.1 eV, pyrrole N at 399.3 eV and graphitic N at 399.9 eV coexist in the BGQD/NCNTs electrocatalyst as shown in Fig. 2a. The bonding between pyridinic N and graphitic N could affect the geometric and electronic structure of the carbon skeleton to exhibit positive function on improving the limiting current density of the catalyst which is favorable for ORR [46]. According to literature, pyridinic N sites are more active toward ORR than those of graphitic N since the latter site would reduce oxygen by a two-electron pathway, whereas the former do it via a four-electron process. Nonetheless, the graphitic N site could offer a remarkable activity for ORR activity as well, resulting from the strong correlations between the amount of graphitic N sites and ORR activity [33]. Furthermore, the absence of B–N bonding in BGQD/NCNTs at 398.2 eV, which might prohibit ORR activity, is expected to result in excellent ORR performance. Meanwhile, the peak at 287.2 eV assigned to the carbon atoms bonding with N further confirms the formation of N-doped graphitic carbon in the C 1s spectrum as Fig. 2b shown. Additionally, as shown in Fig. S4b, the weak XPS B 1s spectra near 192 eV reflects the characteristic of boron, close to boron-carbon environments [47], suggesting that the BGQD were introduced into NCNTs successfully which could providing more active sites toward ORR.

Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS O 1s spectra in Fig. S5 reveal the presence of adsorbed oxygen (AO,
≤
532 eV) and lattice oxygen (LO,
≤
532 eV) in both of NCNTs and BGQD/NCNTs, further interpreting a significant synergistic effect of the BGQD/NCNTs composites toward ORR [39]. In Fig. S5, an increased proportion of LO is observed with the introduction of BGQDs clearly, while pristine NCNTs exhibit a much lower ratio of LO to AO. Several studies have indicated that destroying the electroneutrality of carbon materials could create more charged sites that are favorable for O2 adsorption and improve the activity for ORR of graphitic materials. Notably, carbon atoms with δ+ charge are responsible for increased ORR activity in metal-free carbon electrocatalysts, and LO has a greater capacity to remove electrons from carbon atoms than AO. Consequently, an increased ratio of LO/AO in BGQD/NCNTs could lead to more considerable charge re-distribution further creating more partially positive charged carbon atoms to accelerate ORR [48–50]. In addition, the XRD pattern in Fig. S6 reveals that BGQD was successfully adsorbed into NCNTs further weakening the crystallinity of NCNTs. In addition, Raman spectrum in Fig. 2d indicates two peaks at 1347 and 1572 cm−1 contributed to D-band and G-band, BGQD/NCNTs exhibited a higher ratio of D-band/G-band (ID/IG) of 1.2 than that of pristine NCNTs (ID/IG = 1.0), demonstrating a great number of high disordered carbon with plentiful defects that act as a substantial role in improving electrocatalytic activity for ORR due to their changes in the both of chemical and electrical properties of carbon materials [51]. As a result, it is fair to infer that BGQD/NCNTs were effectively synthesized with the active sites acting as an electrocatalyst for ORR. Importantly, this surface-engineered technique may be extended to synthesize additional heteroatom-doped GQDs using carbon based materials as the matrix, demonstrating its broad applicability.

ORR performance was surveyed from both cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) by operating an instrument of rotating disk electrode (RDE) in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte. Initially, CV curves were performed in N2 and O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. S7, both NCNTs and BGQD/NCNTs show noticeable reduction peaks of oxygen under O2-saturated conditions, suggesting an evident process for oxygen reduction. Furthermore, Fig. 3 a displays the typical LSV curves of different electrocatalysts recorded with a scan rate of 3 mV/s in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution at a rotating speed of 1600 rpm. Impressively, the BGQD/NCNTs catalyst affords the highest onset potential (Eonset) of 0.91 V (vs. RHE), much more positive than CNTs with Eonset of 0.81 V (vs. RHE) and NCNTs with Eonset of 0.86 V (vs. RHE) and also possesses higher diffusion-limited current densities. Barely ORR performance was noticed in bare BGQDs (Fig. S8) due to the utter lack of conductivity [52]. Hence, anchoring BGQDs on a conducive matrix such as NCNT owing to their high electronic conductivity and good stability would be a promising strategy to enhance the ORR activity [53]. As discussed earlier, the pivotal part of the enhanced ORR performance lies in the surface engineered NCNT with BGQDs, providing additional defects as active sites, thus, boosting the ORR performance. In contrast, CNTs and NCNTs exhibit rather inferior ORR activities, indicating that GQDs and heteroatom-doping make major contributions to boost ORR activity as it could provide additional defect as active sites, consistent with the results of Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 2d). However, the excess amount of BGQDs could weaken the ORR performance due to the unfavorable charge transfer of the electrode containing a high percentage of BGQDs owing to the semiconducting nature of BGQDs [39], as shown in Fig. 3b. As a result, the onset potential of BGQD/NCNTs with optimized ratio exceeds that of other BGQD/NCNTs ratios and would be accessible for further utilization electrochemical experiments. Moreover, the ORR performance of the obtained BGQD/NCNTs exceeds most of the previously reported ORR catalysts as listed in Table S1. In addition, the ORR kinetics of BGQD/CNTs was further explored by rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) measurements, as presented in Fig. 3c, and the electron transfer number was also obtained from LSV curves in different rotating speeds from 400 to 1600 rpm. Fig. 3d shows the electron transfer number and yield of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2%) for as-prepared BGQD/CNTs, the value of electron transfer number for BGQD/NCNTs remained between 3.2 and 3.5 for the potentials ranging from 0.2 to 0.6 V, which was quite close to that of reported GQDs-introduced electrocatalysts [54–59]. An analogous tendency for H2O2 yield of BGQD/NCNTs was found from RRDE in the interval of potentials. The results further verify the high selectivity toward 4-electrons transfer pathway dominated ORR process and a promising electrocatalytic activity for BGQD/NCNTs. The electrocatalytic methanol crossover of BGQD/NCNTs was also investigated in Fig. 3e by the chronoamperometry measurement at a constant potential of 0.5 V. Upon the injection of 0.1 M methanol, current density drops sharply in the case of commercial Pt/C, whereas no noticeable changes occur for BGQD/NCNTs, suggesting its excellent methanol tolerance property. Additionally, the durability of BGQD/NCNTs and commercial Pt/C for ORR was then studied in Fig. 3f. BGQD/NCNTs electrocatalyst retained its initial current density up to 93% even after 10,000 s, which is far surpassing 85% of commercial electrocatalysts Pt/C 20% modified electrode after 10,000 s of continuous operation and clearly demonstrating that the much superior durability of the resultant electrocatalysts, confirming its robustness of BGQD/NCNTs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BGQD/NCNTs modified GCE for DO measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
According to electrochemical analysis for ORR, it is fair to confirm that BGQD/NCNTs exhibit a superb electrocatalytic performance for ORR in alkaline medium by a highly effective 4-electron procedure. However, to apply BGQD/NCNTs on electrochemically DO sensing in the whole pH range, ORR performance in neutral and acidic conditions was also investigated in Fig. S9. From Fig. S9, there are apparent reduction peaks in CV curves in both neutral and alkaline mediums, demonstrating its superior electrocatalytic activity for ORR in the board pH range, implying the electrode could conduct DO measurement in various waterbody. Herein, to evaluate the ability of DO measurement for BGQD/NCNTs-modified GCE preliminary, 0.7 M NaCl aqueous solution was prepared to simulate the condition of seawater for DO monitoring. In Fig. 4 a, BGQD/NCNTs modified GCE showed an obvious oxygen reduction peak at 0.55 V (vs. RHE) and cathodic peaks increased gradually with the increment of DO level in seawater, indicating its potential on the field of DO measurement in practical ocean aquacultures. Subsequently, the dynamic concentration range in response to increase levels of oxygen was investigated by LSV and chronoamperograms, as shown in Fig. 4b and c, respectively. Similarly, both results exhibited that cathodic peak current for BGQD/NCNTs GCE raised linearly in the range of 0–18 mg/L oxygen in artificial seawater, which consisted with a trend of CV curves, Fig. 4d shows the calibration curve of DO concentration versus cathodic peak current with a well-defined oxygen concentration dependence with a promising linear response throughout from 0 to 18 ppm, which matched the concentration of DO variation in air-saturated water (
≈
6 ppm under 25 °C) naturally [12]. Furthermore, the sensitivity of BGQD/NCNTs GCE was found to be 0.011 mA/cm2ppm with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99, suggesting its high sensitivity and accuracy for DO measurement. Consequently, it is believed that the capability of utilizing BGQD/NCNTs as a potential non-metal ORR electrocatalyst in DO biosensors with excellent sensitivity for detecting DO in diverse waterbodies across a wide pH range was feasible.

To ensure BGQD/NCNTs modified SPCE could integrate with the wireless communication technologies to realize the day-and-night online monitoring, a two-electrode electrochemical cell with the wireless device was first employed in a beaker containing 1 M KCl aqueous solution with Ag wires as CE and BGQD/NCNTs modified SPCE as WE. Fig. S10 revealed that BGQD/NCNTs still exhibited a highly linear relationship between oxygen concentration and reduction peak current with the sensitivity of 4 μA/cm2ppm in this system. Moreover, the quite close results of sensitivity for BGQD/NCNTs in both positive and reverse direction show its high recovery in DO sensing, implying it could monitor the fluctuation of DO with high accuracy, confirming its possibility to integrate into the remote wireless communication system practically for day-and-night monitoring. Therefore, the results presume BGQD/NCNTs is feasible to assemble with this remote wireless communication device and the conception is illustrated in Fig. 5 a, this device could monitor the variation of oxygen concentration in an aquarium and upload the data to internet pages via WIFI connection in time, achieving the purpose of wireless online-monitoring DO continuous measurement. Fig. 5b presented the actual set-up of proposed system in this work, the wireless device was immersed into 1 M KCl under the temperature of 25 °C, the commercial DO meter was used to affirm the accuracy of this BGQD/NCNTs modified wireless device. The screenshot in Fig. 5c reveals that the result manifests as highly identical with a commercial DO optical sensor, indicating its high accuracy in online DO measurement. The time sequence diagram in Fig. 5d showed that this BGQDs/NCNTs based DO meter can accurately reflect the change of DO in time with the fluctuation of oxygen concentration closely tight with commercial DO meter quantification, implying that it has a high potential to integrate with this wireless communication device online with high accuracy.

In summary, BGQD/NCNTs had successfully been prepared via a facile approach which could substantially diminish the opportunity to form B–N bonding. The results as demonstrated by XPS results, BGQDs were effectively inserted into the NCNTs matrix in the absence of B–N bonding, enhancing the excellent electrocatalytic activity for ORR while maintaining a comparable limiting current and onset potential to pristine NCNTs. The onset potential of BGQD/NCNTs was 0.91 V (vs. RHE) which significantly surpasses pristine CNTs (0.81 V), suggesting its outstanding electrocatalytic activity. The successive incorporation of BGQD into NCNTs lead to the superior ORR performance owing to defect formation upon BGQD incorporation that play as additional active sites for ORR, while NCNTs as conductive pathway are capable of delivering electrons to the electrode with the absence of B–N bonds. Moreover, BGQD/NCNTs composite displays superior long-term stability and methanol interference than commercial Pt/C. Additionally, this work demonstrates the potential of using BGQD/NCNTs as an efficient metal-free catalyst for DO measurement with ultra-high sensitivity in seawater. Importantly, BGQD/NCNTs could be integrated with a wireless communication device to achieve the goal of continuous online DO monitoring, which is advantageous to current intensive fish farming management. According to these results, BGQD/NCNTs could become a considerable prospective electrocatalyst candidate for a novel category of efficient and cost-effective noble metal-free electrocatalysts toward ORR for DO sensing. This synthetic method would be a novel source of inspiration for the development of a variety of highly efficient carbon-based electrocatalysts for a variety of applications.
